articles.headline: NEP 2020 Phase II: Skill Integration and Digital Literacy Drive Launched Nationwide
articles.explainer:
• The Union Education Ministry announced the nationwide rollout of Phase II of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, focusing on vocational skill integration from an early age.
• The initiative aims to bridge the skill gap and enhance employability by integrating practical skills with academic curricula across schools and higher education institutions.
• A key component is the emphasis on digital literacy and computational thinking, preparing students for the future workforce.
• Collaboration with industry partners and local communities will be crucial for providing hands-on training and apprenticeship opportunities.
• Teacher training programs are being revamped to equip educators with the necessary skills to implement the new pedagogical approaches.
prelimsPoints: National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 was approved by the Union Cabinet of India on 29 July 2020.
It replaces the National Policy on Education of 1986.
Key pillars include access, equity, quality, affordability, and accountability.
Focus on multidisciplinary education, critical thinking, and experiential learning.
Vocational education to be integrated into all school and higher education levels.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the potential impact of NEP 2020's skill integration on India's demographic dividend and economic growth.
Discuss the challenges in implementing vocational training and digital literacy programs across diverse educational landscapes in India.
Evaluate the role of government, industry, and educational institutions in achieving the goals of NEP Phase II.
articles.imageDescription: Students engaged in vocational training activities and digital learning labs.
articles.trivia: The NEP 2020 aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Social Issues
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Policy Rates Amidst Global Economic Headwinds, Domestic Inflationary Pressures
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly review, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged at X%.
• The decision comes against a backdrop of persistent global economic uncertainties and elevated domestic food inflation, primarily driven by erratic monsoon patterns.
• RBI highlighted cautious optimism regarding India's growth trajectory but emphasized the need to anchor inflation expectations.
• The central bank reiterated its commitment to supporting liquidity while maintaining financial stability.
• Experts predict a wait-and-watch approach by the RBI, with future rate actions contingent on inflation data and global economic cues.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three officials from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India.
The primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims to keep CPI inflation at 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and growth promotion in the current global and domestic economic scenario.
Discuss the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflationary pressures, especially those arising from food prices.
Evaluate the implications of a 'status quo' monetary policy on various sectors of the Indian economy, including investment and consumption.
articles.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building with financial graphs overlaid.
articles.trivia: The first official interest rate in India was the Bank Rate, introduced in 1935.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economy
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue Concludes: Focus on Maritime Security and Trade Facilitation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue concluded in Jakarta, with both sides reaffirming commitment to a Free, Open, and Inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Key discussions revolved around enhancing maritime security cooperation, combating transnational crimes, and boosting trade and investment.
• India emphasized its 'Act East' policy and its role as a reliable partner in regional peace and prosperity.
• Agreements were reached to fast-track negotiations on the ASEAN-India Free Trade Area (AIFTA) review and promote digital connectivity.
• Cultural exchanges and people-to-people contacts were also highlighted as crucial for strengthening bilateral ties.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) was established in 1967.
India became a Sectoral Dialogue Partner of ASEAN in 1992 and a Full Dialogue Partner in 1995.
India and ASEAN elevated their relationship to a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership in 2022.
The 'Act East' Policy was launched in 2014, succeeding the 'Look East' Policy (1991).
ASEAN members include Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam.
mainsPoints: Examine the significance of ASEAN for India's foreign policy, particularly in the context of the Indo-Pacific strategy and regional security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing economic cooperation between India and ASEAN, with a focus on trade barriers and supply chain resilience.
Evaluate the role of India-ASEAN partnership in addressing common regional challenges such as maritime security, climate change, and transnational crime.
articles.imageDescription: Indian and ASEAN flags displayed together during a diplomatic meeting.
articles.trivia: India is the 4th largest trading partner of ASEAN.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: International Relations
prelimsSyllabus.topic: India and its neighborhood- relations. Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.

articles.headline: Devastating Floods in Northeast India Reignite Debate on Climate Resilience and Disaster Management
articles.explainer:
• Heavy monsoon rains have triggered widespread flooding and landslides across several states in Northeast India, particularly Assam and Meghalaya.
• Thousands have been displaced, and infrastructure severely damaged, prompting calls for urgent humanitarian aid and long-term rehabilitation efforts.
• The recurring nature and increasing intensity of these events highlight the region's vulnerability to climate change impacts.
• Experts are advocating for integrated water management strategies, early warning systems, and climate-resilient infrastructure development.
• The National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) and State Disaster Response Forces (SDRF) are actively engaged in rescue and relief operations.
prelimsPoints: Monsoon in India is primarily influenced by the Differential Heating and Cooling of Land and Water.
The Brahmaputra river system is a major cause of floods in Assam.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) is the apex body for disaster management in India.
NDRF (National Disaster Response Force) is a specialized force for disaster rescue operations.
Climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events globally.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted causes of recurrent floods in Northeast India, including geographical, climatic, and anthropogenic factors.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current disaster management strategies in the region and suggest measures for building long-term climate resilience.
Discuss the socio-economic impacts of floods on vulnerable populations in Northeast India and the role of rehabilitation in sustainable recovery.
articles.imageDescription: Villagers navigating through flooded areas in Assam on makeshift boats.
articles.trivia: The Brahmaputra river is one of the few rivers in the world that exhibits a tidal bore during certain periods.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Geography
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.

articles.headline: ISRO's New Earth Observation Satellite 'Pragyan-2' Launched Successfully, Enhancing Data for Agriculture and Urban Planning
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched 'Pragyan-2', an advanced Earth Observation Satellite (EOS), from Satish Dhawan Space Centre.
• Pragyan-2 is designed to provide high-resolution imagery and data for various applications, including agricultural monitoring, urban development, disaster management, and environmental studies.
• The satellite features enhanced spectral capabilities and improved temporal resolution, allowing for more precise and timely data collection.
• This launch reinforces India's capabilities in remote sensing and its commitment to utilizing space technology for national development.
• The data from Pragyan-2 is expected to significantly benefit government agencies, researchers, and farmers.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
prelimsPoints: ISRO was formed in 1969 and superseded the Indian National Committee for Space Research (INCOSPAR).
Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) are used for mapping, monitoring natural resources, weather forecasting, and disaster management.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's workhorse launch vehicle for placing satellites in polar sun-synchronous orbit.
Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) SHAR, Sriharikota, is India's primary spaceport.
Remote sensing is the acquisition of information about an object or phenomenon without making physical contact with the object.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of Earth Observation Satellites in addressing India's developmental challenges, particularly in agriculture, water resource management, and climate change adaptation.
Analyze the advancements in ISRO's remote sensing capabilities and their strategic implications for national security and economic growth.
Examine the potential benefits and challenges of integrating geospatial data from satellites like Pragyan-2 into government policy-making and public service delivery.
articles.imageDescription: A rocket launching from a launchpad with 'ISRO' logo.
articles.trivia: India is one of the few countries to have successfully launched a mission to Mars (Mangalyaan).

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Science & Technology
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Developments and their applications and effects in everyday life. Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.

articles.headline: Parliament Passes Landmark Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025
articles.explainer:
• The Parliament of India passed the Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025, which aims to provide a comprehensive legal framework for data protection in India.
• The amended bill introduces stricter penalties for data breaches and mandates explicit consent for processing personal data.
• It establishes a Data Protection Board of India to adjudicate on disputes and enforce the provisions of the Act.
• The legislation seeks to balance the rights of individuals to data privacy with the need for organizations to process data for legitimate purposes.
• Critics raise concerns about potential government access to data and the independence of the proposed Data Protection Board.
prelimsPoints: The right to privacy was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 by the Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2017).
The Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee submitted its report on data protection in 2018.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Bill, 2023 was enacted to regulate the processing of digital personal data.
The bill applies to the processing of digital personal data within India and outside India if it relates to offering goods or services to data principals in India.
Penalties for non-compliance can be significant.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025, and its implications for individual privacy and digital governance in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a robust data protection framework in a rapidly digitizing economy, considering issues of consent, data localization, and cross-border data flows.
Critically evaluate the balance struck by the Bill between individual data rights and the legitimate interests of businesses and government, along with concerns regarding state surveillance.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract digital lines representing data flow and security.
articles.trivia: India is home to one of the largest internet user bases in the world.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity & Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

articles.headline: Birth Anniversary of Munshi Premchand: Reaffirming His Relevance in Contemporary Indian Society
articles.explainer:
• July 31st marks the birth anniversary of Munshi Premchand, one of the greatest literary figures of Hindi and Urdu literature.
• His works, including 'Godaan', 'Gaban', and 'Idgah', are celebrated for their realistic portrayal of rural life, social issues, and psychological insights.
• Premchand used his literature as a powerful tool for social commentary, highlighting themes of poverty, exploitation, caste discrimination, and women's empowerment.
• His stories continue to resonate today, offering timeless insights into the human condition and societal challenges that persist in modern India.
• Various literary organizations and cultural institutions are organizing events to commemorate his legacy and discuss his enduring relevance.
prelimsPoints: Munshi Premchand (Dhanpat Rai Srivastava) was born on July 31, 1880, in Lamhi, Varanasi.
He initially wrote under the pen name 'Nawab Rai' in Urdu.
His works are primarily known for their realism and social themes.
Some of his most famous novels include 'Godaan' (The Gift of a Cow), 'Gaban' (Embezzlement), and 'Karmabhoomi'.
He also wrote over 300 short stories, compiled in 'Mansarovar'.
mainsPoints: Analyze how Munshi Premchand's literary works reflected and critiqued the social, economic, and political realities of early 20th-century India.
Discuss the enduring relevance of Premchand's themes of social justice, exploitation, and human dignity in contemporary Indian society.
Evaluate Premchand's contribution to the development of Hindi and Urdu literature and his role as a progressive writer.
articles.trivia: Premchand began his writing career as a school teacher and later worked as a journalist.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: History
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.

articles.headline: Government Boosts Green Hydrogen Ecosystem with New PLI Scheme and Research Grants
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government announced a new Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme specifically for the manufacturing of electrolyzers and components for green hydrogen production.
• This initiative is part of India's broader National Green Hydrogen Mission, aiming to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
• Alongside the PLI scheme, significant research and development grants have been allocated to foster innovation in hydrogen technologies.
• The move is expected to attract substantial investment, create jobs, and accelerate India's transition towards a sustainable energy future.
• Environmental groups welcome the push, emphasizing its potential to reduce carbon emissions and enhance energy security.
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for the production, utilization, and export of Green Hydrogen.
Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
PLI schemes are designed to boost domestic manufacturing and reduce import dependence.
India aims to achieve Net Zero carbon emissions by 2070.
Hydrogen is considered a clean fuel as its combustion only produces water.
mainsPoints: Examine the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change mitigation goals.
Discuss the potential of the new PLI scheme and R&D grants to foster a robust green hydrogen ecosystem in India, including challenges in scaling up production and infrastructure.
Evaluate the economic and environmental benefits of transitioning to a hydrogen-based economy, considering its impact on various industrial sectors.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of hydrogen atoms with green energy symbols.
articles.trivia: India's target for green hydrogen production is 5 million metric tonnes per annum by 2030.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economy
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.

articles.headline: Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) Expanded: Enhancing Surveillance Along Critical Frontiers
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Home Affairs announced the further expansion of the Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) along India's Western borders.
• This phase includes the deployment of advanced surveillance technologies such as thermal imagers, ground sensors, drone systems, and integrated communication networks.
• The goal is to enhance border security, prevent infiltration, and counter cross-border smuggling and terrorism.
• IBMS aims to provide a comprehensive and real-time picture of the border situation to security forces.
• Training programs for Border Security Force (BSF) personnel are being intensified to operate and maintain the new sophisticated equipment.
gsSubTopics: Security Challenges and their Management in Border Areas
Linkages of Organized Crime with Terrorism
Various Security Forces and Agencies and their Mandate
prelimsPoints: IBMS aims to integrate human resources, weapons, and high-tech surveillance equipment.
BSF (Border Security Force) guards India's land borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh.
Smart fencing technologies include laser barriers, radar, sonar, and underground sensors.
The concept of IBMS was proposed after the Kargil War (1999).
It focuses on a multi-layered security system rather than just physical barriers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) in addressing contemporary border security challenges faced by India.
Discuss the technological components of IBMS and their effectiveness in preventing infiltration, smuggling, and cross-border terrorism.
Examine the challenges in implementing and maintaining advanced border surveillance systems, including geographical constraints, human resource requirements, and cyber threats.
articles.imageDescription: Border Security Force personnel patrolling with advanced surveillance drones.
articles.trivia: The BSF is the only Central Armed Police Force to have its own air wing and artillery regiment.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.

articles.headline: India Designates Five New Ramsar Sites, Bolstering Wetland Conservation Efforts
articles.explainer:
• India has added five new wetlands to the list of Ramsar Sites, bringing the total count to a new high.
• These newly designated sites are located across different states, representing diverse ecological characteristics and rich biodiversity.
• The designation underscores India's commitment to the conservation and wise use of its wetlands, which are vital for ecological balance and local livelihoods.
• The Ramsar Convention provides a framework for national action and international cooperation for the conservation and wise use of wetlands and their resources.
• Local communities and forest departments will play a crucial role in the management and protection of these sites.
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: The Ramsar Convention on Wetlands is an intergovernmental treaty for the conservation and sustainable use of wetlands.
It was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971 and came into force in 1975.
India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention.

Wetlands are areas of marsh, fen, peatland or water, whether natural or artificial, permanent or temporary, with water that is static or flowing, fresh, brackish or salt, including areas of marine water the depth of which at low tide does not exceed six metres.
Wetlands provide numerous ecosystem services, including water purification, flood control, and habitat for wildlife.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological and economic significance of wetlands for India, particularly in the context of climate change mitigation and adaptation.
Analyze the challenges in wetland conservation in India, including encroachment, pollution, and lack of awareness, and suggest effective management strategies.
Evaluate the role of international conventions like the Ramsar Convention in strengthening India's domestic efforts for environmental protection and biodiversity conservation.
articles.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds.
articles.trivia: Chilika Lake in Odisha was the first Ramsar site designated in India in 1981.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Environment & Ecology
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.

articles.headline: G20 Sherpas Meet in Delhi to Finalize Agenda for Upcoming Summit, Focus on Global South Priorities
articles.explainer:
• The G20 Sherpas convened in New Delhi for a crucial meeting to finalize the agenda and draft declarations for the upcoming G20 Leaders' Summit.
• Discussions focused on incorporating the priorities of the Global South, including sustainable development, climate finance, and digital public infrastructure.
• India, as the current host, emphasized the need for collective action on global challenges and strengthening multilateralism.
• Key areas of deliberation included economic resilience, food and energy security, and inclusive growth.
• The meeting aimed to build consensus among member nations ahead of the high-level summit.
gsSubTopics: Important International Institutions, agencies and fora
Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The G20 (Group of Twenty) is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development.
The G20 presidency rotates annually among its members.
Sherpas are personal representatives of the leaders of G20 countries.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023, focusing on 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' (One Earth, One Family, One Future).
mainsPoints: Analyze India's role and objectives in hosting the G20 Sherpa meeting, particularly its efforts to amplify the voice of the Global South.
Discuss the significance of G20 in addressing contemporary global challenges such as climate change, economic inequality, and digital transformation.
Evaluate the effectiveness of multilateral forums like G20 in achieving consensus and driving collective action on complex international issues.
articles.imageDescription: G20 logo displayed prominently at a conference venue in Delhi.
articles.trivia: The G20 represents about 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: International Relations
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Validity of Amendments to Election Funding Laws
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India delivered a verdict upholding the constitutional validity of recent amendments to election funding laws, including those related to anonymous corporate donations.
• A five-judge constitutional bench ruled that the amendments, aimed at increasing transparency and curbing black money, do not violate fundamental rights.
• The judgment emphasized the need to balance the right to know of citizens with the privacy of donors and the need for political parties to raise funds.
• The ruling is expected to have significant implications for electoral reforms and political financing in India.
• Dissenting voices from civil society groups expressed concerns about the potential for corporate influence and lack of complete transparency.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
prelimsPoints: The Supreme Court is the highest judicial body in India.
A Constitutional Bench comprises at least five judges.
Electoral bonds were introduced in 2018 as an instrument for political funding.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes the right to information.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) is responsible for conducting elections fairly and transparently.
mainsPoints: Analyze the Supreme Court's rationale in upholding the validity of amendments to election funding laws, specifically regarding the balance between transparency and donor privacy.
Discuss the implications of this judgment on the electoral process, political accountability, and the role of corporate funding in Indian democracy.
Critically evaluate the existing framework of political financing in India and suggest further reforms to enhance transparency and reduce the influence of black money.
articles.trivia: The first Lok Sabha elections in India were held in 1951-52.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity & Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure. Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.

articles.headline: Ethical AI Framework for Public Services: NITI Aayog Releases Draft Guidelines
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog released a draft framework for ethical use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in public services, inviting feedback from stakeholders.
• The guidelines aim to ensure fairness, transparency, accountability, and privacy in AI systems deployed by government agencies.
• Focus areas include bias mitigation, data security, human oversight, and explainability of AI decisions, particularly in sensitive sectors like healthcare and justice.
• This move signals India's proactive approach to AI governance, balancing innovation with ethical considerations.
• The framework is expected to evolve into a comprehensive policy, guiding the development and deployment of responsible AI in the public domain.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) was formed on January 1, 2015, replacing the Planning Commission.
It acts as the premier policy 'Think Tank' of the Government of India.
AI refers to the simulation of human intelligence in machines that are programmed to think like humans and mimic their actions.
Ethical AI principles include fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, and safety.
Bias in AI can arise from biased training data or algorithmic design.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges associated with the deployment of Artificial Intelligence in public services, citing relevant examples.
Examine the key provisions of NITI Aayog's draft ethical AI framework and its potential to foster responsible AI development and deployment in India.
Evaluate the role of government, industry, and civil society in building public trust and ensuring equitable access to AI-powered public services.
articles.imageDescription: Cogs and gears forming a brain, representing AI and ethical decision-making.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Science & Technology
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.

articles.headline: POSHAN Abhiyaan Mid-Term Review Highlights Progress and Challenges in Tackling Malnutrition
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Women and Child Development released the mid-term review report of POSHAN Abhiyaan (National Nutrition Mission).
• The report highlighted significant progress in reducing stunting, wasting, and anemia among children and women in several states.
• However, it also identified persistent challenges related to last-mile delivery, behavioral change communication, and inter-sectoral convergence.
• Recommendations include strengthening Anganwadi services, enhancing community participation, and leveraging technology for better monitoring and evaluation.
• The mission aims to address malnutrition comprehensively through a multi-ministerial approach.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
articles.source: Ministry of Women and Child Development
prelimsPoints: POSHAN Abhiyaan (PM's Overarching Scheme for Holistic Nourishment) was launched in 2018.
It aims to reduce stunting, undernutrition, anemia, and low birth weight.
The mission targets children (0-6 years), adolescent girls, pregnant women, and lactating mothers.
Anganwadi Centres (AWCs) are the focal points for delivering services under the Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) scheme.
The National Family Health Survey (NFHS) provides crucial data on health and nutrition indicators.
mainsPoints: Analyze the achievements and challenges of POSHAN Abhiyaan in addressing the multifaceted problem of malnutrition in India.
Discuss the importance of a multi-sectoral approach and community participation in achieving nutrition security, citing examples from the mission's implementation.
Suggest innovative strategies and policy interventions to overcome the identified challenges and accelerate progress towards India's nutrition targets.
articles.imageDescription: Children receiving nutritious food at an Anganwadi center.
articles.trivia: POSHAN Abhiyaan is a key component of the 'National Nutrition Strategy'.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Social Issues
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

articles.headline: Reviving MSMEs: Credit Guarantee Schemes and Market Access Initiatives Show Positive Impact
articles.explainer:
• A government report indicates that targeted credit guarantee schemes and enhanced market access initiatives have significantly boosted the Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME) sector.
• The schemes have facilitated easier access to finance for MSMEs, reducing their reliance on informal credit sources.
• Initiatives for market linkages, including e-commerce integration and participation in government procurement, have opened new avenues for MSME products.
• The growth in the MSME sector is crucial for job creation, economic diversification, and achieving an inclusive growth trajectory.
• Challenges remain in terms of technological up-gradation, skill development, and navigating regulatory complexities.
prelimsPoints: MSMEs are defined based on investment in plant and machinery/equipment and turnover.
The MSME sector is a significant contributor to India's GDP and employment.
Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises (CGTMSE) provides guarantee cover to banks for collateral-free loans.
Government e-Marketplace (GeM) facilitates online procurement of goods and services by government organizations.
The 'Udyam Registration' portal simplifies the process of registering MSMEs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the MSME sector in driving India's economic growth and employment generation, especially in post-pandemic recovery.
Discuss the effectiveness of government initiatives, such as credit guarantee schemes and market access programs, in addressing the key challenges faced by MSMEs.
Suggest policy measures to further strengthen the MSME ecosystem, focusing on technological up-gradation, skill development, and ease of doing business.
articles.imageDescription: Workers in a small manufacturing unit, symbolizing the MSME sector.
articles.trivia: MSMEs contribute approximately 30% to India's GDP.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economy
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Industrial policy and its effects on industrial growth.

articles.headline: Urban Heat Island Effect: Major Cities Implement Green Infrastructure and Cool Roof Technologies
articles.explainer:
• Several Indian metropolitan cities are actively implementing strategies to mitigate the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect, a phenomenon causing urban areas to be significantly warmer than their surroundings.
• Initiatives include large-scale tree plantation drives, development of urban forests, and promotion of green roofs and cool roofs in construction.
• These measures aim to reduce ambient temperatures, lower energy consumption, and improve air quality in urban environments.
• Municipal corporations are collaborating with urban planners and environmental experts to integrate these solutions into city master plans.
• Public awareness campaigns are also underway to encourage citizen participation in adopting sustainable practices.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
prelimsPoints: The Urban Heat Island effect is a phenomenon where urban areas are significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas.
Causes include heat absorption by dark surfaces (roads, buildings), lack of vegetation, and waste heat from human activities.
Green infrastructure includes practices like urban forests, parks, and green roofs.
Cool roofs are designed to reflect more sunlight and absorb less heat than standard roofs.
The Albedo effect refers to the measure of the reflectivity of a surface.
mainsPoints: Explain the Urban Heat Island effect and its causes in rapidly urbanizing Indian cities, discussing its socio-economic and environmental consequences.
Evaluate the effectiveness of green infrastructure and cool roof technologies as mitigation strategies for the UHI effect, considering their feasibility and scalability in diverse urban contexts.
Discuss the role of urban planning, policy interventions, and citizen participation in building climate-resilient and sustainable cities in India.
articles.imageDescription: A city skyline with lush green rooftops and parks contrasting with concrete buildings.
articles.trivia: Studies show that urban temperatures can be 1-3°C higher than surrounding rural areas during the day, and up to 12°C higher at night.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Geography
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.

articles.headline: Remembering Sardar Udham Singh on His Death Anniversary: A Legacy of Courage and Patriotism
articles.explainer:
• July 31st marks the death anniversary of Sardar Udham Singh, a revolutionary freedom fighter known for assassinating Michael O'Dwyer.
• Udham Singh sought revenge for the Jallianwala Bagh massacre of 1919, which he witnessed as a young man.
• His act of defiance in London in 1940 symbolized India's struggle against colonial oppression and inspired many.
• Historians and political analysts continue to debate the nature of his act – as a revolutionary or a terrorist – but his patriotism remains undisputed.
• Commemorative events are being held to honor his sacrifice and contributions to India's independence movement.
prelimsPoints: Sardar Udham Singh was born on December 26, 1899, in Sunam, Punjab.
He assassinated Michael O'Dwyer, the former Lieutenant Governor of Punjab, in London on March 13, 1940.
O'Dwyer was believed to have approved General Dyer's actions during the Jallianwala Bagh massacre (April 13, 1919).
Udham Singh was hanged on July 31, 1940, at Pentonville Prison, London.
He adopted the name 'Ram Mohammad Singh Azad' to symbolize unity among various Indian communities.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and motivations behind Sardar Udham Singh's actions, placing them within the broader narrative of India's freedom struggle.
Discuss the impact of revolutionary nationalist figures like Udham Singh on the Indian independence movement and their legacy in post-colonial India.
Critically evaluate the different perspectives on Udham Singh's act and its relevance in understanding the complexities of resistance against colonial rule.
articles.imageDescription: A black and white photograph of Sardar Udham Singh.
articles.trivia: Udham Singh visited the US, Africa, and Soviet Union before reaching London.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: History
prelimsSyllabus.topic: The Freedom Struggle — its various stages and important contributors/contributions from different parts of the country.

highlights: NEP 2020 Phase II launches, focusing on skill integration and digital literacy for enhanced employability.
RBI maintains policy rates amid global headwinds and domestic inflation concerns, adopting a cautious stance.
India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue concludes, emphasizing maritime security and trade facilitation in the Indo-Pacific.
Northeast India grapples with devastating floods, prompting renewed calls for climate resilience and disaster management.
Parliament passes Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025, establishing a robust framework for data privacy.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, consider the following statements:
1. It aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
2. It recommends the integration of vocational education from an early age.
3. The policy replaces the National Policy on Education of 1992.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: NEP 2020 aims to increase GER in higher education to 50% by 2035. Statement 2 is correct: A key focus of NEP 2020 is the integration of vocational education from early stages. Statement 3 is incorrect: NEP 2020 replaces the National Policy on Education of 1986, not 1992.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for determining the policy interest rates in India?
options: (b) Reserve Bank of India Board of Directors
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body, headed by the RBI Governor, that determines the policy interest rates required to achieve the inflation target.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Ramsar Convention:
1. It is an intergovernmental treaty for the conservation and sustainable use of wetlands.
2. India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention.
3. The convention was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971 and came into force in 1975.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The Ramsar Convention is indeed an intergovernmental treaty for wetland conservation, India is a signatory, and it was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971 and came into force in 1975.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to Sardar Udham Singh, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. He was a revolutionary freedom fighter who assassinated Michael O'Dwyer.
2. He was a key figure in the Chittagong Armoury Raid.
3. He adopted the name 'Ram Mohammad Singh Azad'.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: Sardar Udham Singh assassinated Michael O'Dwyer. Statement 2 is incorrect: Surya Sen led the Chittagong Armoury Raid. Statement 3 is correct: He adopted the name 'Ram Mohammad Singh Azad' to symbolize unity.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following factors primarily contribute to the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect?
1. High albedo surfaces in urban areas.
2. Lack of vegetation and green spaces.
3. Waste heat generated from human activities.
4. Increased evapotranspiration from urban landscaping.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: Urban surfaces typically have low albedo (absorb more heat). Statement 2 is correct: Lack of vegetation reduces cooling through evapotranspiration. Statement 3 is correct: Industrial activities, vehicles, and air conditioning release heat. Statement 4 is incorrect: Increased evapotranspiration would cool, not heat, the urban environment.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the potential of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's focus on skill integration and digital literacy to transform India's workforce and address the challenges of unemployment and underemployment. What are the key implementation hurdles and how can they be overcome?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources; GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NEP 2020's vision for skill development. Discuss its potential benefits like enhanced employability, demographic dividend utilization, and future-readiness. Identify implementation challenges such as infrastructure, teacher training, industry-academia gap, and funding. Suggest solutions like PPP models, curriculum flexibility, continuous teacher development, and leveraging technology.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the significance of the recently passed Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025, for individual privacy and digital governance in India. To what extent does it balance the rights of data principals with the legitimate interests of data fiduciaries and the state?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; GS3: Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
mainsMocks.approach: Explain the context of data protection in India (Puttaswamy judgment). Detail key provisions of the DPDP (Amendment) Bill, 2025, like consent, data fiduciary obligations, and Data Protection Board. Analyze its implications for individual rights (privacy, consent) and for businesses (compliance, data processing). Critically evaluate the balance with state access and potential for misuse, suggesting improvements.

mainsMocks.question: Examine the multi-faceted causes of recurrent floods in Northeast India, particularly in the context of climate change. What measures are being adopted, or should be adopted, to build long-term climate resilience and improve disaster management strategies in the region?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS1: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes; GS3: Disaster and Disaster Management.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with the geographical vulnerability of Northeast India (Brahmaputra basin, seismic zone). Discuss natural causes (heavy monsoon, topography) and anthropogenic factors (deforestation, river encroachment, poor urban planning). Link to climate change impacts (extreme weather). Outline current measures (NDRF, early warning). Suggest long-term solutions: integrated water management, resilient infrastructure, forest conservation, community-based disaster preparedness, and climate adaptation policies.

quickBytes.text: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims to increase Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India consists of six members: three from RBI and three external members.
India's 'Act East' Policy was launched in 2014, succeeding the 'Look East' Policy.
The Ramsar Convention for wetlands conservation was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971.
ISRO was formed in 1969, superseding INCOSPAR.
The right to privacy was declared a fundamental right by the Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2017).
Munshi Premchand's birth anniversary is observed on July 31st. He wrote in Hindi and Urdu.
India aims to achieve Net Zero carbon emissions by 2070.
The G20 represents about 85% of global GDP and two-thirds of the world population.
Sardar Udham Singh assassinated Michael O'Dwyer in London in 1940 as revenge for Jallianwala Bagh.

GS1.headline: Devastating Floods in Northeast India Reignite Debate on Climate Resilience and Disaster Management
GS1.explainer:
• Heavy monsoon rains have triggered widespread flooding and landslides across several states in Northeast India, particularly Assam and Meghalaya.
• Thousands have been displaced, and infrastructure severely damaged, prompting calls for urgent humanitarian aid and long-term rehabilitation efforts.
• The recurring nature and increasing intensity of these events highlight the region's vulnerability to climate change impacts.
• Experts are advocating for integrated water management strategies, early warning systems, and climate-resilient infrastructure development.
• The National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) and State Disaster Response Forces (SDRF) are actively engaged in rescue and relief operations.
prelimsPoints: Monsoon in India is primarily influenced by the Differential Heating and Cooling of Land and Water.
The Brahmaputra river system is a major cause of floods in Assam.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) is the apex body for disaster management in India.
NDRF (National Disaster Response Force) is a specialized force for disaster rescue operations.
Climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events globally.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted causes of recurrent floods in Northeast India, including geographical, climatic, and anthropogenic factors.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current disaster management strategies in the region and suggest measures for building long-term climate resilience.
Discuss the socio-economic impacts of floods on vulnerable populations in Northeast India and the role of rehabilitation in sustainable recovery.
GS1.imageDescription: Villagers navigating through flooded areas in Assam on makeshift boats.
GS1.trivia: The Brahmaputra river is one of the few rivers in the world that exhibits a tidal bore during certain periods.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Geography
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.

GS1.headline: Birth Anniversary of Munshi Premchand: Reaffirming His Relevance in Contemporary Indian Society
GS1.explainer:
• July 31st marks the birth anniversary of Munshi Premchand, one of the greatest literary figures of Hindi and Urdu literature.
• His works, including 'Godaan', 'Gaban', and 'Idgah', are celebrated for their realistic portrayal of rural life, social issues, and psychological insights.
• Premchand used his literature as a powerful tool for social commentary, highlighting themes of poverty, exploitation, caste discrimination, and women's empowerment.
• His stories continue to resonate today, offering timeless insights into the human condition and societal challenges that persist in modern India.
• Various literary organizations and cultural institutions are organizing events to commemorate his legacy and discuss his enduring relevance.
prelimsPoints: Munshi Premchand (Dhanpat Rai Srivastava) was born on July 31, 1880, in Lamhi, Varanasi.
He initially wrote under the pen name 'Nawab Rai' in Urdu.
His works are primarily known for their realism and social themes.
Some of his most famous novels include 'Godaan' (The Gift of a Cow), 'Gaban' (Embezzlement), and 'Karmabhoomi'.
He also wrote over 300 short stories, compiled in 'Mansarovar'.
mainsPoints: Analyze how Munshi Premchand's literary works reflected and critiqued the social, economic, and political realities of early 20th-century India.
Discuss the enduring relevance of Premchand's themes of social justice, exploitation, and human dignity in contemporary Indian society.
Evaluate Premchand's contribution to the development of Hindi and Urdu literature and his role as a progressive writer.
GS1.trivia: Premchand began his writing career as a school teacher and later worked as a journalist.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: History
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.

GS1.headline: Urban Heat Island Effect: Major Cities Implement Green Infrastructure and Cool Roof Technologies
GS1.explainer:
• Several Indian metropolitan cities are actively implementing strategies to mitigate the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect, a phenomenon causing urban areas to be significantly warmer than their surroundings.
• Initiatives include large-scale tree plantation drives, development of urban forests, and promotion of green roofs and cool roofs in construction.
• These measures aim to reduce ambient temperatures, lower energy consumption, and improve air quality in urban environments.
• Municipal corporations are collaborating with urban planners and environmental experts to integrate these solutions into city master plans.
• Public awareness campaigns are also underway to encourage citizen participation in adopting sustainable practices.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
prelimsPoints: The Urban Heat Island effect is a phenomenon where urban areas are significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas.
Causes include heat absorption by dark surfaces (roads, buildings), lack of vegetation, and waste heat from human activities.
Green infrastructure includes practices like urban forests, parks, and green roofs.
Cool roofs are designed to reflect more sunlight and absorb less heat than standard roofs.
The Albedo effect refers to the measure of the reflectivity of a surface.
mainsPoints: Explain the Urban Heat Island effect and its causes in rapidly urbanizing Indian cities, discussing its socio-economic and environmental consequences.
Evaluate the effectiveness of green infrastructure and cool roof technologies as mitigation strategies for the UHI effect, considering their feasibility and scalability in diverse urban contexts.
Discuss the role of urban planning, policy interventions, and citizen participation in building climate-resilient and sustainable cities in India.
GS1.imageDescription: A city skyline with lush green rooftops and parks contrasting with concrete buildings.
GS1.trivia: Studies show that urban temperatures can be 1-3°C higher than surrounding rural areas during the day, and up to 12°C higher at night.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Geography
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.

GS1.headline: Remembering Sardar Udham Singh on His Death Anniversary: A Legacy of Courage and Patriotism
GS1.explainer:
• July 31st marks the death anniversary of Sardar Udham Singh, a revolutionary freedom fighter known for assassinating Michael O'Dwyer.
• Udham Singh sought revenge for the Jallianwala Bagh massacre of 1919, which he witnessed as a young man.
• His act of defiance in London in 1940 symbolized India's struggle against colonial oppression and inspired many.
• Historians and political analysts continue to debate the nature of his act – as a revolutionary or a terrorist – but his patriotism remains undisputed.
• Commemorative events are being held to honor his sacrifice and contributions to India's independence movement.
prelimsPoints: Sardar Udham Singh was born on December 26, 1899, in Sunam, Punjab.
He assassinated Michael O'Dwyer, the former Lieutenant Governor of Punjab, in London on March 13, 1940.
O'Dwyer was believed to have approved General Dyer's actions during the Jallianwala Bagh massacre (April 13, 1919).
Udham Singh was hanged on July 31, 1940, at Pentonville Prison, London.
He adopted the name 'Ram Mohammad Singh Azad' to symbolize unity among various Indian communities.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and motivations behind Sardar Udham Singh's actions, placing them within the broader narrative of India's freedom struggle.
Discuss the impact of revolutionary nationalist figures like Udham Singh on the Indian independence movement and their legacy in post-colonial India.
Critically evaluate the different perspectives on Udham Singh's act and its relevance in understanding the complexities of resistance against colonial rule.
GS1.imageDescription: A black and white photograph of Sardar Udham Singh.
GS1.trivia: Udham Singh visited the US, Africa, and Soviet Union before reaching London.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: History
prelimsSyllabus.topic: The Freedom Struggle — its various stages and important contributors/contributions from different parts of the country.

GS2.headline: NEP 2020 Phase II: Skill Integration and Digital Literacy Drive Launched Nationwide
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Education Ministry announced the nationwide rollout of Phase II of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, focusing on vocational skill integration from an early age.
• The initiative aims to bridge the skill gap and enhance employability by integrating practical skills with academic curricula across schools and higher education institutions.
• A key component is the emphasis on digital literacy and computational thinking, preparing students for the future workforce.
• Collaboration with industry partners and local communities will be crucial for providing hands-on training and apprenticeship opportunities.
• Teacher training programs are being revamped to equip educators with the necessary skills to implement the new pedagogical approaches.
prelimsPoints: National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 was approved by the Union Cabinet of India on 29 July 2020.
It replaces the National Policy on Education of 1986.
Key pillars include access, equity, quality, affordability, and accountability.
Focus on multidisciplinary education, critical thinking, and experiential learning.
Vocational education to be integrated into all school and higher education levels.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the potential impact of NEP 2020's skill integration on India's demographic dividend and economic growth.
Discuss the challenges in implementing vocational training and digital literacy programs across diverse educational landscapes in India.
Evaluate the role of government, industry, and educational institutions in achieving the goals of NEP Phase II.
GS2.imageDescription: Students engaged in vocational training activities and digital learning labs.
GS2.trivia: The NEP 2020 aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Social Issues
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

GS2.headline: India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue Concludes: Focus on Maritime Security and Trade Facilitation
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue concluded in Jakarta, with both sides reaffirming commitment to a Free, Open, and Inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Key discussions revolved around enhancing maritime security cooperation, combating transnational crimes, and boosting trade and investment.
• India emphasized its 'Act East' policy and its role as a reliable partner in regional peace and prosperity.
• Agreements were reached to fast-track negotiations on the ASEAN-India Free Trade Area (AIFTA) review and promote digital connectivity.
• Cultural exchanges and people-to-people contacts were also highlighted as crucial for strengthening bilateral ties.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) was established in 1967.
India became a Sectoral Dialogue Partner of ASEAN in 1992 and a Full Dialogue Partner in 1995.
India and ASEAN elevated their relationship to a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership in 2022.
The 'Act East' Policy was launched in 2014, succeeding the 'Look East' Policy (1991).
ASEAN members include Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam.
mainsPoints: Examine the significance of ASEAN for India's foreign policy, particularly in the context of the Indo-Pacific strategy and regional security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing economic cooperation between India and ASEAN, with a focus on trade barriers and supply chain resilience.
Evaluate the role of India-ASEAN partnership in addressing common regional challenges such as maritime security, climate change, and transnational crime.
GS2.imageDescription: Indian and ASEAN flags displayed together during a diplomatic meeting.
GS2.trivia: India is the 4th largest trading partner of ASEAN.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: International Relations
prelimsSyllabus.topic: India and its neighborhood- relations. Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.

GS2.headline: Parliament Passes Landmark Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025
GS2.explainer:
• The Parliament of India passed the Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025, which aims to provide a comprehensive legal framework for data protection in India.
• The amended bill introduces stricter penalties for data breaches and mandates explicit consent for processing personal data.
• It establishes a Data Protection Board of India to adjudicate on disputes and enforce the provisions of the Act.
• The legislation seeks to balance the rights of individuals to data privacy with the need for organizations to process data for legitimate purposes.
• Critics raise concerns about potential government access to data and the independence of the proposed Data Protection Board.
prelimsPoints: The right to privacy was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 by the Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2017).
The Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee submitted its report on data protection in 2018.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Bill, 2023 was enacted to regulate the processing of digital personal data.
The bill applies to the processing of digital personal data within India and outside India if it relates to offering goods or services to data principals in India.
Penalties for non-compliance can be significant.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill, 2025, and its implications for individual privacy and digital governance in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a robust data protection framework in a rapidly digitizing economy, considering issues of consent, data localization, and cross-border data flows.
Critically evaluate the balance struck by the Bill between individual data rights and the legitimate interests of businesses and government, along with concerns regarding state surveillance.
GS2.imageDescription: Abstract digital lines representing data flow and security.
GS2.trivia: India is home to one of the largest internet user bases in the world.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity & Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

GS2.headline: G20 Sherpas Meet in Delhi to Finalize Agenda for Upcoming Summit, Focus on Global South Priorities
GS2.explainer:
• The G20 Sherpas convened in New Delhi for a crucial meeting to finalize the agenda and draft declarations for the upcoming G20 Leaders' Summit.
• Discussions focused on incorporating the priorities of the Global South, including sustainable development, climate finance, and digital public infrastructure.
• India, as the current host, emphasized the need for collective action on global challenges and strengthening multilateralism.
• Key areas of deliberation included economic resilience, food and energy security, and inclusive growth.
• The meeting aimed to build consensus among member nations ahead of the high-level summit.
gsSubTopics: Important International Institutions, agencies and fora
Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The G20 (Group of Twenty) is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development.
The G20 presidency rotates annually among its members.
Sherpas are personal representatives of the leaders of G20 countries.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023, focusing on 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' (One Earth, One Family, One Future).
mainsPoints: Analyze India's role and objectives in hosting the G20 Sherpa meeting, particularly its efforts to amplify the voice of the Global South.
Discuss the significance of G20 in addressing contemporary global challenges such as climate change, economic inequality, and digital transformation.
Evaluate the effectiveness of multilateral forums like G20 in achieving consensus and driving collective action on complex international issues.
GS2.imageDescription: G20 logo displayed prominently at a conference venue in Delhi.
GS2.trivia: The G20 represents about 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: International Relations
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Validity of Amendments to Election Funding Laws
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India delivered a verdict upholding the constitutional validity of recent amendments to election funding laws, including those related to anonymous corporate donations.
• A five-judge constitutional bench ruled that the amendments, aimed at increasing transparency and curbing black money, do not violate fundamental rights.
• The judgment emphasized the need to balance the right to know of citizens with the privacy of donors and the need for political parties to raise funds.
• The ruling is expected to have significant implications for electoral reforms and political financing in India.
• Dissenting voices from civil society groups expressed concerns about the potential for corporate influence and lack of complete transparency.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
prelimsPoints: The Supreme Court is the highest judicial body in India.
A Constitutional Bench comprises at least five judges.
Electoral bonds were introduced in 2018 as an instrument for political funding.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes the right to information.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) is responsible for conducting elections fairly and transparently.
mainsPoints: Analyze the Supreme Court's rationale in upholding the validity of amendments to election funding laws, specifically regarding the balance between transparency and donor privacy.
Discuss the implications of this judgment on the electoral process, political accountability, and the role of corporate funding in Indian democracy.
Critically evaluate the existing framework of political financing in India and suggest further reforms to enhance transparency and reduce the influence of black money.
GS2.trivia: The first Lok Sabha elections in India were held in 1951-52.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity & Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure. Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.

GS2.headline: POSHAN Abhiyaan Mid-Term Review Highlights Progress and Challenges in Tackling Malnutrition
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Women and Child Development released the mid-term review report of POSHAN Abhiyaan (National Nutrition Mission).
• The report highlighted significant progress in reducing stunting, wasting, and anemia among children and women in several states.
• However, it also identified persistent challenges related to last-mile delivery, behavioral change communication, and inter-sectoral convergence.
• Recommendations include strengthening Anganwadi services, enhancing community participation, and leveraging technology for better monitoring and evaluation.
• The mission aims to address malnutrition comprehensively through a multi-ministerial approach.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
GS2.source: Ministry of Women and Child Development
prelimsPoints: POSHAN Abhiyaan (PM's Overarching Scheme for Holistic Nourishment) was launched in 2018.
It aims to reduce stunting, undernutrition, anemia, and low birth weight.
The mission targets children (0-6 years), adolescent girls, pregnant women, and lactating mothers.
Anganwadi Centres (AWCs) are the focal points for delivering services under the Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) scheme.
The National Family Health Survey (NFHS) provides crucial data on health and nutrition indicators.
mainsPoints: Analyze the achievements and challenges of POSHAN Abhiyaan in addressing the multifaceted problem of malnutrition in India.
Discuss the importance of a multi-sectoral approach and community participation in achieving nutrition security, citing examples from the mission's implementation.
Suggest innovative strategies and policy interventions to overcome the identified challenges and accelerate progress towards India's nutrition targets.
GS2.imageDescription: Children receiving nutritious food at an Anganwadi center.
GS2.trivia: POSHAN Abhiyaan is a key component of the 'National Nutrition Strategy'.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Social Issues
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Policy Rates Amidst Global Economic Headwinds, Domestic Inflationary Pressures
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly review, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged at X%.
• The decision comes against a backdrop of persistent global economic uncertainties and elevated domestic food inflation, primarily driven by erratic monsoon patterns.
• RBI highlighted cautious optimism regarding India's growth trajectory but emphasized the need to anchor inflation expectations.
• The central bank reiterated its commitment to supporting liquidity while maintaining financial stability.
• Experts predict a wait-and-watch approach by the RBI, with future rate actions contingent on inflation data and global economic cues.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three officials from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India.
The primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims to keep CPI inflation at 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and growth promotion in the current global and domestic economic scenario.
Discuss the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflationary pressures, especially those arising from food prices.
Evaluate the implications of a 'status quo' monetary policy on various sectors of the Indian economy, including investment and consumption.
GS3.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building with financial graphs overlaid.
GS3.trivia: The first official interest rate in India was the Bank Rate, introduced in 1935.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economy
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.

GS3.headline: ISRO's New Earth Observation Satellite 'Pragyan-2' Launched Successfully, Enhancing Data for Agriculture and Urban Planning
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched 'Pragyan-2', an advanced Earth Observation Satellite (EOS), from Satish Dhawan Space Centre.
• Pragyan-2 is designed to provide high-resolution imagery and data for various applications, including agricultural monitoring, urban development, disaster management, and environmental studies.
• The satellite features enhanced spectral capabilities and improved temporal resolution, allowing for more precise and timely data collection.
• This launch reinforces India's capabilities in remote sensing and its commitment to utilizing space technology for national development.
• The data from Pragyan-2 is expected to significantly benefit government agencies, researchers, and farmers.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
prelimsPoints: ISRO was formed in 1969 and superseded the Indian National Committee for Space Research (INCOSPAR).
Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) are used for mapping, monitoring natural resources, weather forecasting, and disaster management.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's workhorse launch vehicle for placing satellites in polar sun-synchronous orbit.
Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) SHAR, Sriharikota, is India's primary spaceport.
Remote sensing is the acquisition of information about an object or phenomenon without making physical contact with the object.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of Earth Observation Satellites in addressing India's developmental challenges, particularly in agriculture, water resource management, and climate change adaptation.
Analyze the advancements in ISRO's remote sensing capabilities and their strategic implications for national security and economic growth.
Examine the potential benefits and challenges of integrating geospatial data from satellites like Pragyan-2 into government policy-making and public service delivery.
GS3.imageDescription: A rocket launching from a launchpad with 'ISRO' logo.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the few countries to have successfully launched a mission to Mars (Mangalyaan).

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Science & Technology
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Developments and their applications and effects in everyday life. Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.

GS3.headline: Government Boosts Green Hydrogen Ecosystem with New PLI Scheme and Research Grants
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government announced a new Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme specifically for the manufacturing of electrolyzers and components for green hydrogen production.
• This initiative is part of India's broader National Green Hydrogen Mission, aiming to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
• Alongside the PLI scheme, significant research and development grants have been allocated to foster innovation in hydrogen technologies.
• The move is expected to attract substantial investment, create jobs, and accelerate India's transition towards a sustainable energy future.
• Environmental groups welcome the push, emphasizing its potential to reduce carbon emissions and enhance energy security.
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for the production, utilization, and export of Green Hydrogen.
Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
PLI schemes are designed to boost domestic manufacturing and reduce import dependence.
India aims to achieve Net Zero carbon emissions by 2070.
Hydrogen is considered a clean fuel as its combustion only produces water.
mainsPoints: Examine the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change mitigation goals.
Discuss the potential of the new PLI scheme and R&D grants to foster a robust green hydrogen ecosystem in India, including challenges in scaling up production and infrastructure.
Evaluate the economic and environmental benefits of transitioning to a hydrogen-based economy, considering its impact on various industrial sectors.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized image of hydrogen atoms with green energy symbols.
GS3.trivia: India's target for green hydrogen production is 5 million metric tonnes per annum by 2030.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economy
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.

GS3.headline: Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) Expanded: Enhancing Surveillance Along Critical Frontiers
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Home Affairs announced the further expansion of the Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) along India's Western borders.
• This phase includes the deployment of advanced surveillance technologies such as thermal imagers, ground sensors, drone systems, and integrated communication networks.
• The goal is to enhance border security, prevent infiltration, and counter cross-border smuggling and terrorism.
• IBMS aims to provide a comprehensive and real-time picture of the border situation to security forces.
• Training programs for Border Security Force (BSF) personnel are being intensified to operate and maintain the new sophisticated equipment.
gsSubTopics: Security Challenges and their Management in Border Areas
Linkages of Organized Crime with Terrorism
Various Security Forces and Agencies and their Mandate
prelimsPoints: IBMS aims to integrate human resources, weapons, and high-tech surveillance equipment.
BSF (Border Security Force) guards India's land borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh.
Smart fencing technologies include laser barriers, radar, sonar, and underground sensors.
The concept of IBMS was proposed after the Kargil War (1999).
It focuses on a multi-layered security system rather than just physical barriers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) in addressing contemporary border security challenges faced by India.
Discuss the technological components of IBMS and their effectiveness in preventing infiltration, smuggling, and cross-border terrorism.
Examine the challenges in implementing and maintaining advanced border surveillance systems, including geographical constraints, human resource requirements, and cyber threats.
GS3.imageDescription: Border Security Force personnel patrolling with advanced surveillance drones.
GS3.trivia: The BSF is the only Central Armed Police Force to have its own air wing and artillery regiment.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.

GS3.headline: India Designates Five New Ramsar Sites, Bolstering Wetland Conservation Efforts
GS3.explainer:
• India has added five new wetlands to the list of Ramsar Sites, bringing the total count to a new high.
• These newly designated sites are located across different states, representing diverse ecological characteristics and rich biodiversity.
• The designation underscores India's commitment to the conservation and wise use of its wetlands, which are vital for ecological balance and local livelihoods.
• The Ramsar Convention provides a framework for national action and international cooperation for the conservation and wise use of wetlands and their resources.
• Local communities and forest departments will play a crucial role in the management and protection of these sites.
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: The Ramsar Convention on Wetlands is an intergovernmental treaty for the conservation and sustainable use of wetlands.
It was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971 and came into force in 1975.
India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention.

Wetlands are areas of marsh, fen, peatland or water, whether natural or artificial, permanent or temporary, with water that is static or flowing, fresh, brackish or salt, including areas of marine water the depth of which at low tide does not exceed six metres.
Wetlands provide numerous ecosystem services, including water purification, flood control, and habitat for wildlife.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological and economic significance of wetlands for India, particularly in the context of climate change mitigation and adaptation.
Analyze the challenges in wetland conservation in India, including encroachment, pollution, and lack of awareness, and suggest effective management strategies.
Evaluate the role of international conventions like the Ramsar Convention in strengthening India's domestic efforts for environmental protection and biodiversity conservation.
GS3.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds.
GS3.trivia: Chilika Lake in Odisha was the first Ramsar site designated in India in 1981.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Environment & Ecology
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.

GS3.headline: Ethical AI Framework for Public Services: NITI Aayog Releases Draft Guidelines
GS3.explainer:
• NITI Aayog released a draft framework for ethical use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in public services, inviting feedback from stakeholders.
• The guidelines aim to ensure fairness, transparency, accountability, and privacy in AI systems deployed by government agencies.
